entry.title: Updates to T4A-NR Withholding Tax Report (Canada)
entry.content:
Introduction

Each year, the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) updates the T4A-NR form, summary, and associated electronic filing requirements to reflect evolving tax laws, improve clarity, and address user feedback. For the 2025 tax filing season, CRA has implemented several important changes, impacting how businesses report payments to non-residents for services rendered in Canada.

Understanding the T4A-NR Slip: A Refresher

Before we dive into the changes, let's quickly recap the importance of the T4A-NR slip. This critical tax form is used to report amounts paid to non-residents for services rendered in Canada, helping ensure accurate income reporting, compliance, and reliable tax filing for non-residents.

Both the T4A-NR slip and the T4A-NR-SUM form, which summarizes the total amounts reported on all T4A-NR slips for each recipient, are subject to updates, including changes to their layout, instructions, and required information.

Updates to the T4A-NR, T4A-NR-SUM Forms:

While the T4A-NR form (24) saw no changes to the tax boxes themselves, there were updates to the completion guidelines for:

  • T4A-NR Withholding Tax Slip (FIWTCA_T4ANR_INT_SLIP)  
  • T4A-NR Withholding Tax summary Slip (FIWTCA_T4ANR_SUMMARY_SLIP)
  • T4A-NR Withholding Tax Slip (FIWTCA_T4ANR_SLIP)

Additionally, electronic filing became mandatory for businesses submitting more than 5 information returns, a significant reduction from the previous threshold of 50. The changes ensure an improvement to the accuracy and efficiency of reporting payments made to non-residents for services performed in Canada. 

Key Updates Electronic Transmittal XML Filing:

The T619 XML file, used for electronically transmitting various tax return types (including the T4A-NR), now requires adherence to significantly revised XML filing requirements, designed to enhance data security and integrity:

  • XML Schema Updates:  The CRA has implemented stricter XML schema validation rules. This requires comprehensive data field population and validation before submission, significantly reducing the risk of rejected filings.
  • Field Changes: Pay close attention to the following specific field changes:
    • Fields Removed: Key identifiers such as Report Type Code and Transmitter number have been eliminated.
    • Fields Updated: Transmitter name and Transmitter Country Code adjustments for precision.
    • New Fields: Newly introduced fields include TransmitterAccountNumber and TransmitterRepID, demanding attention to configuration.

Note: all returns filed in the same submission must be the same information return type. For example, a T4A return cannot be submitted together with a T4 return in the same electronic file.

 Why These Changes Matter

Failing to use the correct T4A-NR form or adhering to the updated XML filing requirements can result in:

  • Penalties: The CRA can impose penalties for non-compliance with tax regulations.
  • Processing Delays: Incorrect or incomplete filings can lead to delays in processing tax returns, both for your business and the non-residents you are paying.
  • Audit Risks: Non-compliance can increase the likelihood of a CRA audit.

How SAP Helps Businesses Stay Compliant 

SAP's Globalization team supports customers using SAP S/4HANA powered by SAP DRC to address regulatory changes by:

  1. Software Updates: SAP delivers regular updates and support packages to ensure that your SAP software reflects the latest legal changes, including updates to XML formats and form layouts.
  2. Documentation: Refer to the SAP Help Portal documentation and SAP Note 3559241 for details of the recent changes to the T4A-NR Report.
  3. Expert Support: SAP offers multiple support channels, including expert chat, community forums, and scheduled sessions with SAP experts, to provide timely assistance with any questions or challenges.
  4. Localization Services: SAP's Globalization Services team ensures that SAP solutions are compliant with Canadian laws and regulatory standards. Leverage the Regulatory Change Manager for agility in adapting to new legal requirements. Furthermore, the SAP Road Map Explorer offers insights into forthcoming updates and feature enhancements.

 Conclusion

The CRA's regular updates to tax and reporting requirements can be challenging for businesses. SAP's proactive and responsive approach helps customers manage these changes effectively. By utilizing SAP S/4HANA powered by SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC), businesses can maintain compliance, minimize errors, and optimize their tax reporting processes.

Review the CRA's official T4A-NR guidelines and stay informed through the SAP Localization community page for ongoing updates, valuable resources, and expert discussions.

entry.title: Unlocking Innovation: Understanding SAP's Clean Core and Its Benefits (for IT Leaders)
entry.content:
Headline: Future-Proofing Your SAP: An IT Leader's Guide to the Clean Core Revolution

Introduction:

In today's fast-moving world, IT leaders face a constant battle: driving innovation while keeping their mission-critical ERP systems stable. For those of you running SAP, you know this struggle all too well. The traditional approach of endless customization, while solving short-term needs, has often led to a tangled web of complexity, making upgrades a nightmare and hindering agility. But there's a better way forward – a story of reclaiming control and empowering innovation: the Clean Core revolution.

The Legacy Trap: A Cautionary Tale of Customization Chaos

Let's paint a familiar picture. You're the CIO of a growing enterprise, and your SAP system, once a powerful engine, has become a labyrinth of custom code. Over decades, countless modifications, tweaks, and add-ons have been layered on, each addressing a specific need but collectively creating a monster.

  • Upgrades? They're not routine updates; they're major projects, consuming months and millions, with no guarantee of success.
  • Maintenance? A huge chunk of your budget is spent not on innovation, but on keeping this fragile system alive.
  • New features? Business users demand them to stay competitive, but your IT team is stuck in firefighting mode, struggling to keep the core from collapsing.

This was the reality for many IT leaders: a world where their ERP system, meant to be a strategic asset, became a costly liability. The culprit? Over-customization.

A New Hope: The Clean Core Philosophy

But a new approach has emerged, a philosophy that's empowering IT leaders to break free from this trap: Clean Core.

Clean Core isn't just a technical fix; it's a fundamental shift in how we think about and manage SAP. It's the recognition that the core SAP system – the engine that drives your essential business processes – should be kept as standardized as possible. Think of it as the bedrock of your IT strategy, providing stability and reliability.

Instead of directly altering this core, Clean Core advocates for extending its functionality through clearly defined, decoupled extensions, primarily on platforms like the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). This means adding new capabilities alongside the core, not within it.

The Clean Core Promise: Agility, Innovation, and Control

So, what does Clean Core offer the IT leader?

  • Unlocking Agility: In today's world, businesses need to adapt at lightning speed. A clean core allows you to respond quickly to changing market demands, new business models, and emerging technologies. You can roll out new features and applications without the fear of destabilizing your core systems.
  • Fueling Innovation: Clean Core provides a stable foundation for innovation. By decoupling extensions, you create a safe space for experimentation and the adoption of cutting-edge technologies like AI, machine learning, and advanced analytics.
  • Reclaiming Control: Clean Core empowers IT leaders to regain control over their SAP landscapes. You move from a reactive mode of constantly fixing issues to a proactive stance of shaping your IT destiny. Upgrades become less daunting, maintenance becomes more manageable, and you can finally focus on strategic initiatives.
  • Reducing TCO: By minimizing modifications, upgrades become less disruptive, faster, and more cost-effective.

The Clean Core Playbook: Key Implementation Strategies

How do IT leaders bring this vision to life? Here’s your playbook:

  1. Embrace Standardization: The first principle of Clean Core is to adhere to standard SAP functionality as much as possible. This means resisting the urge to customize the core to fit every minor variation in your business processes.
  2. Strategic Extensions: When extensions are necessary, deploy them on SAP BTP. This platform allows you to build and run applications and integrations without touching the core, ensuring upgradeability and flexibility.
  3. API-First Approach: Prioritize the use of modern, stable APIs (like OData and SOAP) for integration, rather than relying on older, more invasive methods.
  4. Data Excellence: Maintain impeccable data quality. This means ensuring accuracy, completeness, and consistency while also controlling data volume and adhering to data privacy regulations.
  5. Clean Code Practices: Implement rigorous custom code management practices. This includes using tools like SAP Cloud ALM and SAP Readiness Check to manage and remediate custom code, and adopting agile development methodologies with thorough documentation and testing.
  6. Seamless Deployment: Streamline the deployment of extensions by integrating Cloud TMS and BTP CI/CD, ensuring a smooth and consistent release process.
  7. Strategic Governance: Establish a robust governance framework to enforce Clean Core principles across all IT projects. This includes analyzing existing customizations, embracing SAP BTP, and continuously seeking opportunities to optimize and simplify your SAP landscape.

The Enablers: SAP Activate and SAP Cloud ALM

SAP provides powerful tools and methodologies to support your Clean Core journey:

  • SAP Activate: This methodology provides a structured framework for SAP S/4HANA implementations and transformations, with Clean Core at its heart. It offers best practices, guided configurations, and project management tools to ensure a smooth transition.
  • SAP Cloud ALM: This suite of tools helps you manage the entire lifecycle of your SAP landscape, from implementation to operations. It provides capabilities for monitoring, testing, change management, and integration, all crucial for maintaining a Clean Core.

The AI Revolution: Supercharging Clean Core with Joule

The emergence of artificial intelligence is further accelerating the Clean Core revolution. Tools like Joule, SAP’s AI copilot within SAP Build, are streamlining the development process. Joule assists developers in writing code, designing workflows, and automating tasks, making the creation of clean core-compliant extensions faster and more efficient.

The Future is Clean: An IT Leader's Call to Action

Clean Core isn't just a technical best practice; it's a strategic imperative for IT leaders who want to future-proof their SAP investments and drive sustainable growth. By embracing Clean Core principles, leveraging SAP's cutting-edge tools, and fostering a culture of standardization and innovation, you can transform your SAP landscape from a source of complexity into an engine of agility and competitive advantage. The future of SAP is clean, agile, and ready for anything – are you?

entry.title: How Tech Envo migrated 32 SAP SuccessFactors HCM integrations from Neo to Cloud Foundry in 5 weeks
entry.content:
1. Purpose of the document:

This document outlines how Tech Envo successfully migrated 32 SAP SuccessFactors HCM integrations from Neo to Cloud Foundry within a five-week timeframe. The doc covers the planning, migration approach, deployment, Lessons learned, and Tech Envo Recommendations. 

The customer has built 32 integrations in 2016-17 using the SAP Cloud Integration (SAP CPI) Neo environment for SAP SuccessFactors HCM. My customer plans to start the S/4HANA journey in September 2025, and SAP Integration Suite is the chosen integration platform. We will utilize all SAP IS capabilities. Hence, moving to Cloud Foundry makes sense.


2. Why SAP SuccessFactors HCM customer needs to migrate from Neo to Cloud Foundry SAP Integration Suite: 

The multi-cloud foundation has become the strategic focus for SAP BTP due to its significant benefits compared to the Neo environment, including its principles of openness and freedom of choice in terms of support for multiple infrastructure providers, data centers, and a wide range of runtimes and programming languages. Unlike for Neo where you can use selected service

  1. Many SAP customers have built integrations in the Neo SAP Cloud Integration (Cloud Integration) environment; now, SAP encourages customers to move to the Multi-Cloud Foundation.
  2. SAP Business Technology Platform, Neo environment will sunset on December 31, 2028.
  3. SAP Cloud Foundry Multi Cloud Foundation offers all the services provided by SAP Integration Suite 
  4. It is a strategic decision to migrate to Multi Cloud Foundation.

 3. Neo to CF SAP IS Migration Steps by Tech Envo:


4. Migration Decision-Making Process: 

We have utilized this migration opportunity to enhance the readability of the SAP IS Packages. This is an optional step, but we highly recommend it. 

  Migration Step 1: Cloud Foundry SAP Integration Suite Provisioning: 

  1.     Get access to the Cloud Foundry Environment
  2.     Set up Sub Account
  3.     Set up Admin, Roles, and Authorizations
  4.     Identify the volume of messages and check the right license model selected
  5.     Detailed steps for pre-requisites are mentioned in the help documentation below

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-integration/sap-cloud-integration/initial-setup-of-sap-cloud-integration-in-cloud-foundry-environment  

Migration Step 2: Neo Environment Assessment and Evaluation: 

  1. Create a detailed assessment report capturing all the details about the Neo Tenant Integrations
  2. Validated naming conventions used in the Neo environment, and decided to make some changes after migration to add a unique ID to the package and a meaningful name for value mappings
  3. Performed cleanup of unused integrations and excluded them from the Migration
  4. Shared CF IP ranges with external systems to whitelist the traffic

Below is the inventory sheet we generated out of the new environment

Migration Step 3: Migration Planning  and Tasks: 

  1.     Create a detailed task that needs to be executed as part of the migration. 
  2.     Execute all pre-requisites ahead of migration. 
  3.     Identify which tasks will be automated using the SAP Migration tool, and which tasks need to be performed manually

Migration Step 4: Migrate Integration to CF Dev environment:

  • We have used the SAP Migration Assistance Tool and the Manual  Approach to complete the Migration. Many blogs have been written that have step-by-step instructions on configuring the Migration Assistance tool.

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-integration/sap-cloud-integration-migration-guide/migrating-cloud-integration-from-neo-environment-to-multi-cloud-foundation 

  • Identified what objects can be migrated using the SAP Automation Tool and what requires a manual approach.
  • Executed migration and Validation Tasks with Duration and status tracking and performed smoke testing to ensure the end-to-end connection worked for each integration.
  • Check and configure Run Time parameters and ensure everything is moved and configured. 
  • Executed all integrations in the Dev environment, ensuring they executed successfully.

 Migration Step 5: Production Migration and Deployment

  1. Start Production Migration once the Dev environment tasks and validation have been successfully migrated. 
  2. Migrate all Integration Content from Neo Production to SAP IS Production Tenant.
  3. Perform Validation for each interface and do smoke testing (if necessary)
  4. Stop Neo Schedules
  5. Schedule all production interfaces in the SAP IS environment. 

Migration Step 6: Decommission of Neo Tenant:

  1.  Plan the decommissioning of the SAP Neo Tenant after 4 weeks of SAP IS Live.
  2. Notify SAP Operations to decommission the tenant

5. Lessons Learned Migrating SAP SuccessFactors HCM Interfaces from Neo to CF :

  1.  Neo Dev Tenant has so many unused packages – We identified the Final Integration packages based on the Production Deployed Interfaces
  2. The Integration code misalignment—The dev and Product codebases are not the same, and the customer team made changes directly in the code. We migrated code from Neo Prod to Neo Dev and ensured that everything was identical to production.
  3. Naming conventions: The SI (don’t want to reveal the name), who built the integrations in Neo, did not follow any naming conventions. Hence, we decided to add a unique ID and make the interface package name meaningful for custom integrations. 
  4. The SAP Cloud Connector Changes: Setting up the cloud connector to connect the CF SAP IS sub-account took longer due to the customer governance process.   
  5. IP Allowlisting and External Systems Connection: Plan sufficient time to ensure all connections to external systems and IP whitelisting are made. 
  6. Authentication Mechanism: Most integrations have used basic authentication, which is high-risk. We utilized this migration opportunity to address the issue.

6. Tech Envo Recommendations for Neo to CF Migration:

  •  The Neo to SF SAP IS Migration effort is less, so plan it better and document the tasks.
  • Get the right SI/consultants who have experience with Neo to CF Migration. Your project shouldn’t become a playground for learning and performing migration tasks.
  • Look for an opportunity to make Integration packages more readable, like having meaningful names.
  • Implement automation to receive email notifications when an interface fails and certificates expire. Tech Envo has built two integrations to handle the same. 
  • The migration follows a sequence of steps mentioned above; make sure to follow the sequence in the correct order to ensure a successful migration.
  • Maintain a good documentation for each integration, including a technical specification document with detailed technical information, an interface design diagram, and an overall landscape.
  • Implement the proper security authentication mechanism and encryption techniques

entry.title: Optimizing Inventory Flow: SAP EWM Replenishment Process
entry.content:
In the competitive landscape of supply chain management, effective inventory management is crucial for ensuring that products are available when needed, minimizing stockouts, and maintaining optimal operational efficiency. SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) provides a robust framework for managing inventory replenishment processes, ensuring that stock levels are maintained across various storage locations. This article will explore the EWM replenishment process in detail, outlining its significance, key components, and best practices for successful implementation.

What is Replenishment?

Replenishment is the process of moving products from bulk storage areas to picking locations to ensure that inventory levels meet demand. In a warehouse context, this means ensuring that fast-moving items are readily accessible to pickers, thereby optimizing picking efficiency and reducing order fulfillment times.

Replenishment Strategies:

  1. Planned Replenishment
  2. Automatic Replenishment
  3. Crate Part Replenishment
  4. Order-Based / Demand-Based Replenishment
  5. Direct / Manual / Picker-Driven Replenishment

Create Storage types

Created 2 new storage types REP1 & REP2 for this replenishment example, one for replenishment at Storage Type level and one at Bin level. Also created few bins in each storage type using Create Storage Bin transaction /SCWM/LS01.

Please note that, reason for creating separate storage types for this example is only just to keep is isolated with other scenarios for better understanding, else you can update “Putaway Rule” & “Repl. level” of your existing storage types according to your replenishment configurations requirements.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Master Data > Define Storage Types

For replenishment storage types REP1 at Storage Type Level selected Putaway Rule = 4 (Gen Storage Area) & Repl. level = 1 (Storage Type level)

For replenishment storage types REP2 at Storage Type Level selected Putaway Rule = 4 (Addition to existing bin) & Repl. level = “ “ (Storage Bin level)

Create Warehouse Process Type

Created replenishment warehouse process type referring SAP standard process Type “3010” and selected warehouse process category = 3 (Internal Warehouse Movement)

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Task > Define Warehouse Process Type

And selected Activity = REPL (Replenishment)

Also selected document & item types as SRPL (Replenishment) under warehouse requests settings section

Determine Warehouse Process Type

Configured warehouse process determination for Document & Item Types “SRPL” to use our replenishment process type 3010

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Task > Determine Warehouse Process Type

Activate Replenishment Strategies in Storage Types

For this example activated Planned Replenishment strategies for our new storage types.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Internal Warehouse Processes > Replenishment Control > Activate Replenishment Strategies in Storage Types

Activated Planned Replenishment strategies for Warehouse Process Type 3010 that we are using, and selected Physical Quantity based replenishment along with “WT Immed” checkbox for Immediate Warehouse Task creation.

Maintain Document/Item Categories for Replenishment

Maintained configuration for document & item types SPRL for our warehouse process type 3010 that we are using in this replenishment example.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Internal Warehouse Processes > Replenishment Control > Maintain Document/Item Categories for Replenishment Warehouse Request

Customize Stock Transfer Process

For this example we are not making any changes in standards settings, but in case you have specific requirements to create document type or item type, or to use any custom action profile, status profile or partner profile etc., that can be done using below below configuration.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Internal Warehouse Processes >Delivery Processing > Stock Transfers > Define Document Types for the Stock Transfer Process

Specify Stock Removal Rules

For this example we will use FIFO rule for stock removal.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Strategies > Specify Stock Removal Rules

Specify Storage Type Search Sequence

Defined new storage type search sequence “REPL” for replenishment process.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Strategies > Specify Storage Type Search Sequence

And assigned new storage types REP1 & REP2 in sequence.

Define Stock Removal Control Indicator

Defined new Stock Removal Control Indicator “RELP” for replenishment.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Strategies > Define Stock Removal Control Indicator

Determine Storage Type Search Sequence for Stock Removal

Maintained configuration for Determining Storage Type using new storage type search sequence “REPL” for warehouse process type 3010, using Stock Removal Control Indicator “RELP” and utilizing FIFO rule for stock removal.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Strategies > Determine Storage Type Search Sequence for Stock Removal

Test Your Replenishment Configurations

Let us perform Bin Leve replenishment using configuration we made. For this example we will use sample product 240074 that has 5 pieces in bin IOWA-01–01–07.

So we assign Min 6 & Max 10 quantities for these bin using transaction /SCWM/BinMAT in bin IOWA-01–01–07, so that current quantity “5” is below the minimum configured quantity at bin level.

Now verify the product configurations including Stock Removal configuration changed to use new Stock Removal indicator “REPL” we created for this example.

Note: In real scenario you may be using existing stock removal indicator and may not be creating new indicator as we created for this example to avoid impact on other scenarios test data.

Now just run replenishment for this test product using transaction /SCWM/REPL selecting planned replenishment.

As expected replenishment executed for additional 5 pieces to make total 10 pieces in IOWA-01–01–07 to match the max configured quantity at bin level.

After reviewing replenishment data select the row and execute.

I intentionally unchecked immediate warehouse creation checkbox “WT Immed” to create warehouse request so that I can show creation of further warehouse tasks manually using transaction /SCWM/IM_ST in this example below Internal Stock Transfer #6101300 was created.

Before creating warehouse task I just added some sock in our replenishment bin as that will be used for this replenishment here, and then created warehouse task.

And after confirming the created task you will notice warehouse order like below is created for replenishment of 5 pieces to Bin IOWA-01–01–07 according to the configurations.

And after confirming this warehouse order you will notice replenishment is complete and Bin IOWA-01–01–07 now has 10 pieces that we configured as max quantity for this bin.

In this example we covered Bin Level Replenishment, similarly you can also configure Storage Type level Min Max at product level using /SCWM/MAT1 transaction.  

Best Practices for Effective Replenishment in SAP EWM

  1. Utilize Automated Processes: Leverage SAP EWM’s automation capabilities to streamline replenishment order creation and task assignment, reducing manual intervention and errors.
  2. Implement Dynamic Slotting: Use dynamic slotting strategies to adjust the placement of items based on demand patterns, ensuring that high-velocity items are stored in easily accessible locations.
  3. Collaborative Planning: Foster collaboration between inventory management, sales, and warehouse teams to ensure alignment on demand forecasts and replenishment strategies.
  4. Regular Training: Provide regular training to warehouse staff on the replenishment process, system updates, and best practices to ensure they are equipped to handle replenishment tasks efficiently.
  5. Review and Adjust Strategies: Regularly review replenishment strategies and thresholds to ensure they remain effective in response to changing demand patterns and business conditions.

Conclusion

The replenishment process in SAP EWM is a critical component of effective warehouse management, directly impacting inventory availability, operational efficiency, and customer satisfaction. By following a structured approach to replenishment, from demand forecasting to continuous monitoring and optimization, organizations can ensure that their warehouses operate smoothly and efficiently.

Implementing best practices and leveraging the capabilities of SAP EWM will enable organizations to streamline their replenishment processes, reduce costs, and enhance overall supply chain performance. As businesses continue to adapt to the dynamic nature of the market, mastering the replenishment process in SAP EWM will be essential for achieving operational excellence and sustained competitive advantage.

entry.title: Bypassing CDS Filters in SADL-Based OData Services for Flexible Fiori Applications
entry.content:
In modern SAP Fiori applications, developers often rely on CDS views exposed via SADL (Service Adaptation Definition Language) to build quick and efficient OData services. While this architecture promotes reusability and low-code development, it can become restrictive when CDS views include hardcoded filters or mandatory parameters. 

IO_TECH_REQUEST_CONTEXT in GET_EXPANDED_ENTITYSET is an interface that provides: 

1️⃣Request details (keys, headers, and $expand navigation properties) 

2️⃣Access to OData query options like filters and expansions 

3️⃣Contextual control for fetching a main entity with its related data in one call  

(Think of it as a "request assistant" that tells your OData service exactly what data to return and how.) 

/IWBEP/IF_MGW_REQ_ENTITYSET   

🔍Implementing Query Options Using /IWBEP/IF_MGW_REQ_ENTITYSET 

The interface /IWBEP/IF_MGW_REQ_ENTITYSET is a core component of SAP NetWeaver Gateway's code-based OData service implementation. It is primarily used within the GET_ENTITYSET method of the Data Provider Extension Class (DPC_EXT) to handle query options such as filtering, sorting, and paging. 

📌Key Query Option Methods 

This interface provides several methods to retrieve and handle query parameters passed from the client: 

  • get_filter_string( ) 
    Retrieves the raw filter string from the request URI. Useful for logging or manual parsing if needed. 
  • get_filter( ) 
    Returns a structured filter object that can be further analyzed using standard methods. 
  • get_filter_select_options( ) 
    Converts the filter into a select-option compatible internal table (/iwbep/t_mgw_select_option). This is particularly useful when passing filters to BAPIs, function modules, or for building dynamic WHERE clauses in ABAP. 
  • get_top( ) and get_skip( ) 
    Retrieve the $top and $skip values used for client-side paging. 
  • get_orderby( ) 
    Provides sorting information from the $orderby clause. 
  • has_filter( ) 
    Checks whether a filter condition was provided in the request.

If  want know more Implementing Query Options 

To enable value help functionality in a CDS view, we first define the necessary annotations. Specifically, we use: 

@search.defaultSearchElement: true 

@Consumption.valueHelpDefinition:[{ entity:{ element: 'MATNR' ,name: 'ZI_SEARCH_MARA2' } }] 

In this context, ZI_SEARCH_MARA2 is a separate CDS view that serves as the value help entity.

In the CDS view ZI_SEARCH_MARA2, the annotation @ObjectModel.dataCategory: #VALUE_HELP is mandatory for enabling the view to function as a value help source in Fiori applications. Without this annotation, the CDS view will not be recognized by the Fiori framework as a valid value help provider, and the associated value help functionality will not work as expected

Registering CDS as OData Data Source 

  1. Right-click on your OData project 
  2. Select Referenced Data Sources 
  3. In the dialog: 
  4. Choose "Core Data Services" tab 
  5. Click "Add..." button 
  6. Search for your CDS view (Z_MATERIALMASTER) 
  7. Select and confirm

Now consuming the OData in SAP Fiori Generator by using the VS code by choosing the list report page  

We can Preview Application . 

Find the Entity Name From UI 

  • Open Browser Developer Tools 
  • Press F12 or Ctrl+Shift+I (Chrome/Edge) 
  • Go to the Network tab 
  • Trigger the Value Help 
  • Click on the value help icon in your Fiori app 
  • Watch for new network requests to appear 
  • Locate the Batch Request ($batch) 
  • Filter requests by typing "$batch" in the search box 
  • Look for the most recent POST request to .../$batch 
  • Inspect the Payload 
  • Click on the $batch request 
  • Go to the "Payload" tab (or "Request Payload") 
  • Look for lines starting with GET followed by entity names like: GET EntitySetName?$filter=...

Locating the EntitySet Method for Value Help in OData Projects 

Step-by-Step Guide to Find the Correct Method 

  • After Identifying the Entity Name (from browser network inspection) 
  • Example: You found entity Z_C_EntityName in the $batch payload 
  • Navigate to Your OData Project in ADT/Eclipse 
  • Open your OData service project 
  • Expand the "Runtime Artifacts" folder 
  • Open the DPC_EXT Class 
  • Look for ZCL_[YourServiceName]_DPC_EXT 
  • This is your custom extension class that inherits from the generated DPC class 
  • Find the EntitySet Method 
  • Search for methods with naming pattern:[ENTITYSET_NAME]_GET_ENTITYSET  
  • For our example Z_C_ProductVH, look for:Z_C_PRODUCTVH_GET_ENTITYSET

1. Set Breakpoints: 

  1. Put a breakpoint in the method and trigger the value help again 
  2. Examine the io_tech_request_context contents during debugging 

2.Check Base Class: 

  1. If not found in DPC_EXT, check the parent DPC class 
  2. Generated methods might be in the base class 

3. Common Patterns: 

  1. Value help methods often: 
  2. Join multiple tables 
  3. Implement special search algorithms 
  4. Have _GET_ENTITYSET suffix 

4. Related Methods: 

  1. Sometimes value help uses both: 
  2. ..._GET_ENTITYSET for the list 
  3. ..._GET_ENTITY for single record validation

This approach helps you quickly locate and modify the backend logic powering your Fiori app's value help functionality.

 

Now we can implement code here. 

By Redefending the method  

 

data(lv_filter) = io_tech_request_context->get_filter( )->get_filter_string( ). 
  
if lv_filter is NOT INITIAL. 
  
filter_string_url( 
   EXPORTING 
     iv_filter_string = lv_filter 
   IMPORTING 
     et_entityset     = ET_ENTITYSET 
 ). 
 
data(lv_filter) = io_tech_request_context->get_filter( )->get_filter_string( ).
  1. io_tech_request_context 
    The gateway request object containing all OData call details (URL, headers, queries). 
  2. ->get_filter() 
    Accesses the $filter clause from the request, returning a filter object reference. 
  3. ->get_filter_string() 
    Extracts the raw filter condition as a string (e.g., "(Price gt 100) and (Category eq 'Electronics')"). 
  4. Key Purpose 
    Gets the unprocessed filter exactly as sent by the client for debugging or custom parsing. 

filter_string_url This method is used to custom filtering implementation is written which returns the entityset based on the lv_filters 

Lv_filter gives  values like (   ( MATNR = ‘ 1 ’  )  or ( MATNR = ‘ 7 ’  ) ) 

  METHOD filter_string_url. 
     DATA:lt_Matnr_range TYPE RANGE OF mara-Matnr, 
          ls_Matnr_range LIKE LINE OF lt_Matnr_range. 
  
    IF iv_filter_string IS NOT INITIAL. 
  
      DATA:lv_string TYPE string. 
       DATA:lt_string TYPE TABLE OF string. 
  
      lv_string = iv_filter_string. 
  
      REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF '(' IN lv_string WITH ''. 
       REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF ')' IN lv_string WITH ''. 
       REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF `'` IN lv_string WITH ''. 
       REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF '&' IN lv_string WITH 'AND'. 
       REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF 'and' IN lv_string WITH 'AND'. 
       REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF 'le' IN lv_string WITH ''. 
       REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF 'ge' IN lv_string WITH ''. 
       REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF 'MATNR' IN lv_string WITH ''. 
       REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF '=' IN lv_string WITH ''. 
  
      TRANSLATE lv_string TO UPPER CASE. 
  
      SPLIT lv_string AT 'AND' INTO TABLE lt_string. 
       SPLIT lv_string AT 'OR' INTO TABLE lt_string. 
  
      SORT lt_string. 
       CLEAR lv_string. 
       READ TABLE lt_string INTO lv_string INDEX 1. 
       IF sy-subrc = 0. 
         CONDENSE lv_string. 
         CALL FUNCTION 'CONVERSION_EXIT_MATN1_INPUT' 
           EXPORTING 
             input        = lv_string 
           IMPORTING 
             output       = ls_Matnr_range-low 
           EXCEPTIONS 
             length_error = 1 
             OTHERS       = 2. 
       ENDIF. 
  
      CLEAR lv_string. 
       IF lines( lt_string ) > 1. 
         READ TABLE lt_string INTO lv_string INDEX lines( lt_string ). 
         IF sy-subrc = 0. 
           CONDENSE lv_string. 
           CALL FUNCTION 'CONVERSION_EXIT_MATN1_INPUT' 
             EXPORTING 
               input        = lv_string 
             IMPORTING 
               output       = ls_Matnr_range-high 
             EXCEPTIONS 
               length_error = 1 
               OTHERS       = 2. 
           ENDIF. 
       ENDIF. 
       IF ls_Matnr_range-high IS NOT INITIAL. 
         ls_Matnr_range-sign = 'I'. 
         ls_Matnr_range-option = 'BT'. 
       ELSE. 
         ls_Matnr_range-sign = 'I'. 
         ls_Matnr_range-option = 'EQ'. 
       ENDIF. 
  
      APPEND ls_Matnr_range TO lt_Matnr_range. 
       CLEAR ls_Matnr_range. 
  
 
      IF lt_Matnr_range IS NOT INITIAL. 
         SELECT Matnr FROM mara INTO TABLE et_entityset WHERE Matnr IN lt_Matnr_range. 
       ENDIF. 
  
    ENDIF. 
   ENDMETHOD.

 

Conclusion:-

This approach provides the flexibility to: 

  • Bypass restrictive CDS view filters when justified 
  • Implement custom business logic for value help 
  • Maintain control over data exposure 
  • Improve user experience with dynamic filtering 

Remember that while these techniques offer powerful customization options, they should be used judiciously to maintain system stability and security.

entry.title: Adding buttons and redirecting to a tcode in TMG(SM30).
entry.content:
Scenario: 

In a custom development scenario, a Table Maintenance Generator (TMG) was implemented for a custom database table (ZSAG_MARA). During the record creation or editing process in the TMG screen, there was a frequent need to cross-reference data from another database table (ZREFERENCE_TABLE) for validation or lookup purposes. 

To streamline the process and avoid manual navigation to SE16N, a custom button was added to the TMG application toolbar using the GUI status enhancement. This button enables direct navigation to the data browser screen for the referenced table. 

 

Technical Implementation: 

Table: 

TMG creation screen: 

Create a TMG event:

And choose the status for your screen (you can find it in system-> status ->GUI). 

Click on User Interface and copy the program into your program. (you can find it in your target screen where you want to create custom button : system->status-> program GUI): 

Create a custom button(Naviagtion😞 

Function Attributes:

 

  • Now the button should be added in Table view Maintenance: Initial screen. 
  • For adding the logics for ‘Navigation’ button. 
  • Go to maintenance screens. 

Add a module in PAI(ZSAG_POPUP).

Added logic in 'ZSAG_POPUP' as per my requirement is: 

MODULE zsag_popup INPUT.

 data: lv_ans TYPE c.
  case sy-ucomm.
    when 'NAVIGATION'.
      CALL FUNCTION 'POPUP_TO_CONFIRM'
        EXPORTING
         TITLEBAR                    = 'Confirm Action'
          text_question               = 'Do you want to redirect to these Tcode?'
         TEXT_BUTTON_1               = TEXT-001
         ICON_BUTTON_1               = TEXT-002
         TEXT_BUTTON_2               = TEXT-003
         ICON_BUTTON_2               = TEXT-004
         DEFAULT_BUTTON              = '1'
         DISPLAY_CANCEL_BUTTON       = 'X'
       IMPORTING
         ANSWER                      = LV_ANS
       EXCEPTIONS
         TEXT_NOT_FOUND              = 1
         OTHERS                      = 2.
      IF sy-subrc <> 0.
          clear sy-ucomm.
      ENDIF.
     ENDCASE.
ENDMODULE.

 In SM30: (Button should be added (Navigation)).

Click on 'Navigation' button:( A popup will appear).

  • Function code for these buttons will be ‘OPT1’ & ‘OPT2’. 
  • To debug popup You have to drag and drop a txt file on those buttons with this codes should be written inside. 
[Function] 

Command=/H 

Type=SystemCommand

To write a logic we have to create another Module in PAI (ZSAG_Redirect).

MODULE zsag_redirect INPUT.
case sy-ucomm.
  when 'OPT1'.
    CALL TRANSACTION 'SE14'.
  when 'OPT2'.
    call  TRANSACTION 'SE16N'.
ENDCASE.
ENDMODULE.

 After clicking on Tcode ‘SE16N’, It will navigate to Database utility. 

 

Or If user clicks on Tcode ‘SE14’, it will navigate to  ‘Data Browsing or General Table Display’. 

 

Conclusion:

By integrating direct navigation to Data Browser (SE16N) and Database Utilities (SE14) within the TMG screen: 

  • Users save time and avoid context switching. 
  • Enhances data validation and table administration during record maintenance. 
  • Delivers a more user-friendly and efficient TMG interface for functional users and administrators. 

 

Reference:  How to create custom button in TMG - SAP Community

entry.title: Re: Updates to T4A-NR Withholding Tax Report (Canada)
entry.content: The Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) has updated the T4A-NR form and related filing processes for the 2025 tax season, including changes to XML filing requirements. Key updates include stricter validation rules for electronic submissions, new fields, and the requirement for businesses to file electronically if submitting more than 5 returns. These changes aim to improve accuracy and reduce penalties for non-compliance. SAP offers support through software updates and expert assistance to ensure businesses stay compliant with these regulatory changes.

entry.title: Representation of multiple contracts of employees along with pay components
entry.content: Hello Compensation experts! Our client has decided for different reasons (tax purposes etc.) to sign different contracts with employees that may be moving along different countries. For some cases, a user might have even up to 4 contracts (the main one with Greece and three more with England, Ghana & Cyprus). Ηaving so many different contracts, they need a proper way of recording all this info in the system so they can report on it. In the beginning they just wanted to record the different salaries, therefore we decided to create multiple pay components with distinctive names so they know from the code for which country is the contract for. Now they have extended their request and they want to be able to report on the following information: country, employment class, employment type, legal entity, amount, currency, individual/non individual etc. for EVERY contract that a user might have. Global assignment and concurrent employment features cannot serve this purpose. Therefore we would like to ask if any of you has faced any similar request in the past and how you decided to move forward. Maybe custom fields in the pay component recurring section? Or a custom portlet to maintain all this info? But we need to take into consideration the compensation module that will start to be developed in the following months.
